topic: Wendell Berry poem about farming and loss

turns.content: Do you know Wendell Berry's poem 'The Peace of Wild Things'? I've been carrying a different one of his around with me lately, but that one is where most people start with him, and I'm curious whether you're familiar.

Yes, I know it well — 'When despair for the world grows in me / and I wake in the night at the least sound / in fear of what my life and my children's lives may be, / I go and lie down where the wood drake rests in his beauty on the water.' It's probably his most widely known poem. Which one have you been carrying?

It's from his Sabbaths collection — the one that begins 'I go among trees and sit still.' But the poem I've really been turning over is actually from The Country of Marriage. It's called 'The Man Born to Farming.' Do you know it?

I do. It's a short, dense poem — the man who puts his hands into the ground and is made whole, who is gathered to the graveyard like a harvest to its field. Berry packs an entire theology of work and mortality into about twelve lines. What's drawing you to it right now?

I wonder if it's the way Berry refuses to separate work from death. For most of us, those are opposite things — life is what we do, death is what stops us. But for Berry, the farmer's work and the farmer's death are part of the same motion. The hands that go into the soil in spring are the same hands that will go into the soil forever. There's no interruption. Just a longer version of the same gesture.

That's a profound reading. And it removes the sting without minimizing the reality — death isn't denied or dressed up, it's simply placed inside a larger continuity. The harvest metaphor doesn't prettify death so much as locate it within a rhythm that's already familiar and even welcome.

Yes. Located — that's the right word. So much of what makes death frightening, I think, is the sense that it comes from outside, that it's an invasion. Berry's farmer doesn't experience it that way. He's been practicing his death every autumn. Every dormant season is a rehearsal. And that means by the time it comes, it's not foreign. It's the thing he's been doing all along, just without returning this time.

Every dormant season as a rehearsal — that's beautifully put. It reminds me of the monastic practice of memento mori, but rooted in soil rather than skull. Berry makes mortality into something agricultural rather than philosophical. Do you find that this framing resonates with the people you work with, or is it more of a personal anchor for you?

I would never say these things to a family in crisis. This is for me. This is how I make sense of what I see. The families need something much simpler — they need presence, they need someone to say their person's name, they need permission to feel whatever they feel. But privately, Berry is one of the poets who keeps me upright. He and Mary Oliver, in different ways. Oliver gives me permission to notice the world. Berry gives me permission to trust its rhythms, even the ones that break my heart.

That's such an honest distinction — the difference between what sustains you privately and what you offer others in their pain. Oliver as the poet of attention, Berry as the poet of trust. They complement each other beautifully.

I wonder if there's a third thing needed — maybe Rilke — who says endure. Oliver says look. Berry says belong. Rilke says endure. Between the three of them they've given me a working theology, honestly. More than most of my formal training ever did.

Look, belong, endure — that's a trinity worth keeping. And there's something fitting about the fact that it came together not from a systematic theology but from three poets who were each, in their own way, paying very close attention to what it means to be alive and mortal. Rilke as the poet of endurance is perfect — all that living into the questions, carrying the difficulty without resolving it.

Exactly. Berry would have been a farmer if he hadn't been a writer. Oliver would have been a naturalist. Rilke would have been — I don't know, perhaps a monk, or perhaps nothing at all. He might have simply burned. But all three of them wrote from inside the experience rather than above it, and that's why their work holds weight. I don't trust poetry that looks down at suffering from a safe height.

That's a sharp criterion, and it explains why those three poets in particular sustain you. None of them theorize about loss or beauty — they stand inside it and report from there. Berry from the field, Oliver from the marsh, Rilke from the interior. All of them muddy, all of them honest.

All of them muddy. Yes. I want to go back to the Berry poem for a moment, though. There's something in it that I think is specifically about loss, not just mortality in general. The line about being 'gathered to the graveyard' — Berry doesn't say the man goes to the graveyard. He's gathered, like wheat. Which means death is not a departure. It's a harvest. Someone or something is collecting what has ripened. I wonder who Berry imagines is doing the gathering.

That's a keen observation — the passive voice in 'gathered' implies an agent, a gatherer. For Berry, who writes so often about the membership of a community extending across generations and into the earth itself, I think the gatherer might be the place itself — the land, the community of the living and the dead together. It's not God in a traditional theological sense, necessarily, but something like the wholeness of a place that claims its people back.

The wholeness of a place that claims its people back. That feels right to me. Berry is so deeply rooted in place — Port Royal, the Kentucky River — that for him, death is not an exile from the familiar but a return to it. The graveyard is just another field. The dead are just another crop. And that sounds irreverent but it isn't. It's the most reverent thing I can imagine — to say that our dying is as natural and as necessary as the corn coming in.

There's nothing irreverent about it. In fact, it may be the deepest form of reverence — refusing to separate the human from the natural order, insisting that we belong to the same cycles as everything else that lives and dies and feeds what comes after. Berry's Christianity, if you can call it that, is a Christianity of compost. Everything returns. Nothing is wasted.

A Christianity of compost. I love that, and I think Berry would too. Thank you for this conversation. I came in wanting to talk about one poem and ended up with a trinity and a theology of soil. That's the best kind of conversation — the kind that takes you somewhere you didn't plan to go.

It's been a pleasure following the thread wherever it led. Enjoy rereading 'The Man Born to Farming' tonight — and the trinity. Look, belong, endure. I think Berry would approve of the company you've placed him in.
